title: Docker Intro
content:
Docker Introduction

What is Docker?

Docker is an open-source platform that enables developers to build, ship, and run applications in isolated environments called containers.



  • Containers: Lightweight, standalone, and executable packages that include everything needed to run an application (code, runtime, system tools, libraries, and settings).
  • Isolation: Containers run in isolation from each other and the host system, ensuring consistency across different environments.
  • Efficiency: Docker containers share the host's OS kernel, making them much faster and more resource-efficient than traditional Virtual Machines (VMs).

title: Images & Containers
content:
Images vs. Containers

The Building Blocks

To understand Docker, you must understand the relationship between Images and Containers.



  • Docker Image: A read-only template with instructions for creating a Docker container. Think of it as a "blueprint" or a class in OOP.
  • Docker Container: A runnable instance of an image. You can create, start, stop, move, or delete a container using the Docker API or CLI.
Docker CLI Basics
# Pull an image from Docker Hub
docker pull nginx

# Run a container from an image
docker run -d -p 80:80 --name my-web nginx

# List running containers
docker ps

title: Dockerfile
content:
Creating a Dockerfile

Automating Image Creation

A Dockerfile is a text document that contains all the commands a user could call on the command line to assemble an image.

Example Dockerfile (Node.js)
# Use an official Node.js runtime as a parent image
FROM node:18

# Set the working directory in the container
WORKDIR /app

# Copy package.json and install dependencies
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm install

# Copy the rest of the application code
COPY . .

# Expose the port the app runs on
EXPOSE 3000

# Command to run the application
CMD ["node", "index.js"]

title: Docker Hub
content:
Docker Hub

Sharing Your Images

Docker Hub is the world's largest library and community for container images. It's where you find official images for databases, web servers, and languages.



  • Public Repositories: Share your images with the world for free.
  • Official Images: High-quality, secure images maintained by Docker and the software authors.
  • Automated Builds: Automatically build images from GitHub or Bitbucket.
Pushing to Docker Hub
# Log in to Docker Hub
docker login

# Tag your image
docker tag my-image username/my-image:v1.0

# Push the image
docker push username/my-image:v1.0

title: Docker Compose
content:
Docker Compose

Multi-Container Applications

Compose is a tool for defining and running multi-container Docker applications. With Compose, you use a YAML file to configure your application's services.

docker-compose.yml Example
version: '3.8'
services:
  web:
    build: .
    ports:
      - "5000:5000"
  redis:
    image: "redis:alpine"

Run docker-compose up to start your entire application with a single command.

title: Docker Volumes
content:
Persisting Data

Managing Data in Containers

By default, all files created inside a container are stored on a writable container layer. When a container is deleted, the data is lost. Volumes are the preferred mechanism for persisting data.



  • Volumes: Managed by Docker and stored on the host filesystem.
  • Bind Mounts: Map a specific directory on the host to a directory in the container.
Using Volumes
# Create a volume
docker volume create my-db-data

# Run a container with a volume
docker run -d \
  --name dev-db \
  -v my-db-data:/var/lib/mysql \
  mysql:latest

title: Docker Network
content:
Container Networking

Connecting Containers

Docker's networking subsystem allows containers to communicate with each other and with the host machine.



  • Bridge: The default network driver. Best for applications running in standalone containers.
  • Host: Removes network isolation between the container and the Docker host.
  • Overlay: Connects multiple Docker daemons together and enables swarm services to communicate.
Creating a Network
# Create a user-defined bridge network
docker network create my-net

# Run containers on the same network
docker run -d --name db --network my-net mysql
docker run -d --name app --network my-net my-app

title: Best Practices
content:
Docker Best Practices

Efficiency and Security

To get the most out of Docker, follow these industry standards and best practices.



  • Keep Images Small: Use lightweight base images like alpine.
  • Use Multi-stage Builds: Separate build-time dependencies from the final production image.
  • One Process per Container: Each container should have a single responsibility.
  • Avoid Root Privileges: Run your application as a non-root user for better security.
  • Layer Caching: Order commands from least to most likely to change to optimize build times.

title: Docker Architecture
content:
Docker Architecture

The Client-Server Model

Docker uses a client-server architecture. The Docker client talks to the Docker daemon, which does the heavy lifting of building, running, and distributing your Docker containers.



  • The Docker Daemon (dockerd): Listens for Docker API requests and manages Docker objects such as images, containers, networks, and volumes.
  • The Docker Client (docker): The primary way that many Docker users interact with Docker. When you use commands such as docker run, the client sends these commands to dockerd.
  • Docker Desktop: An easy-to-install application for your Mac, Windows or Linux environment that enables you to build and share containerized applications and microservices.

title: Container Lifecycle
content:
Container Lifecycle

States of a Container

A Docker container goes through several states from creation to deletion.



  • Created: Container is created but not started.
  • Running: Container is actively running its primary process.
  • Paused: All processes in the container are suspended.
  • Exited: Container's primary process has finished or was stopped.
  • Dead: Container that failed to stop properly.
Lifecycle Commands
# Start a stopped container
docker start my-container

# Stop a running container
docker stop my-container

# Pause/Unpause
docker pause my-container
docker unpause my-container

title: Executing Commands
content:
Docker Exec

Running Commands in Containers

Sometimes you need to run a command inside a container that is already running. This is often used for debugging or managing services.

Interactive Shell
# Open a bash shell inside a running container
docker exec -it my-container bash

# Run a single command without entering
docker exec my-container ls -l /var/www/html

title: Monitoring & Logs
content:
Docker Logs & Stats

Observability

Monitoring your containers is crucial for debugging performance issues and verifying application behavior.



  • docker logs: Fetch the logs of a container. Useful for seeing application output.
  • docker stats: Display a live stream of container(s) resource usage statistics (CPU, Memory, Network I/O).
  • docker inspect: Return low-level information on Docker objects.
Monitoring Commands
# Follow logs in real-time
docker logs -f my-container

# See resource usage
docker stats

# Inspect container IP address
docker inspect -f '{{range .NetworkSettings.Networks}}{{.IPAddress}}{{end}}' my-container

title: Env Variables
content:
Environment Variables

Configuring Containers

Environment variables are the standard way to pass configuration settings to applications running inside containers.

Passing Variables
# Pass a single variable
docker run -e DB_PASSWORD=secret my-app

# Use an environment file
docker run --env-file .env my-app

title: Multi-stage Builds
content:
Multi-stage Builds

Optimizing Image Size

Multi-stage builds allow you to use multiple FROM statements in your Dockerfile. Each FROM instruction can use a different base, and each of them begins a new stage of the build.

Go Multi-stage Example
# Build Stage
FROM golang:1.21-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /build
COPY . .
RUN go build -o main .

# Final Stage
FROM alpine:latest
WORKDIR /root/
COPY --from=builder /build/main .
CMD ["./main"]

title: Security Scanning
content:
Docker Security

Scanning for Vulnerabilities

Security is a critical part of the container lifecycle. Docker provides tools to scan your images for known vulnerabilities (CVEs).



  • Docker Scout: Analyzes image contents and generates a report on security vulnerabilities.
  • Content Trust: Ensures that users can verify the integrity and the publisher of all the data received from a registry over any channel.
Scanning Images
# Scan an image for vulnerabilities
docker scout quickview my-image:latest

# Get detailed CVE report
docker scout cves my-image:latest

title: Orchestration
content:
Docker Orchestration

Scaling Containers

When your application grows and spans multiple hosts, you need an orchestration tool to manage container lifecycles, scaling, and networking across the cluster.



  • Docker Swarm: Native clustering for Docker. Easy to use and integrated into the Docker CLI.
  • Kubernetes (K8s): The industry standard for container orchestration. More complex but highly flexible and powerful.

Orchestrators handle self-healing (restarting failed containers), load balancing, and rolling updates.

title: Docker Summary
content:
Docker Summary 🐳

You're Ready to Containerize!

Congratulations! You've learned the fundamentals of Docker, from images and containers to Compose and volumes.

  • Ship Anywhere: Containers ensure your app runs the same on your laptop, CI/CD, and production.
  • Microservices: Docker is the backbone of modern microservices architecture.
  • Scalability: Easily scale your applications by running multiple instances of your containers.

Next Steps: Try containerizing one of your own projects, explore Docker Swarm, or dive into Kubernetes for full-scale orchestration!
